summary: I was born and raised in Enumclaw, Washington until I was seven years old. It’s a small place about 20 miles east of Auburn and on the way to Mount Rainier. There was a small Japanese community there, with about eight families, and many worked at the local sawmill or on railroad tracks to bring lumber from the forest. My father worked at the sawmill, and my mother cooked for the bachelors living in a separate barracks. I have fond memories of playing by the pond and swimming with other kids, though there weren’t many my age.

When I turned seven, my family moved to Auburn, Washington because my grandparents had a laundry business there but got too old to run it. My mother took over running the laundry after they passed away, while my father continued working at the sawmill in Enumclaw during the week. We lived in Auburn from age seven until I was eleven, when we were forced to relocate. Auburn was where I went to school and had some Caucasian friends, and I remember my teacher, Miss Louis, even came to see me off when I left for camp. It was a modest but stable childhood despite the hard work my parents did.

Mount Rainier was near where I grew up in Enumclaw, Washington. The town was on the way to the mountain, and it was part of the beautiful natural surroundings of my childhood. I remember the Japanese community living there, working in the sawmill and on railroad tracks in the forests near Mount Rainier. It was a peaceful place before the war disrupted our lives. The mountain was a constant landmark in the background of my early years.

Both my parents were born in Hiroshima, Japan. They immigrated to the United States before I was born, settling in Washington state. Their roots in Hiroshima were part of our family history, though I grew up entirely in the U.S. The connection to Hiroshima became more poignant after the war started, especially with the discrimination we faced. It was a reminder of where my parents came from and the complex feelings during the war.

I remember hearing about the attack on Pearl Harbor on December 7th, 1941. I was helping a Caucasian friend sell newspapers on the street when the news broke. At that moment, I didn’t fully grasp the impact or connect it to my Japanese heritage. My parents, however, were worried about the discrimination that would follow. It was the beginning of a very difficult time for our family and community.

After Pearl Harbor, the FBI came to our house and store to check everything out. They confiscated certain items, including my toy samurai sword, which was very upsetting for me as a child. The FBI raids were traumatic and made it clear that we were being watched and suspected. This was part of the growing tension and discrimination against Japanese Americans before we were relocated.

When we were forced to relocate, we first went to the Pinedale Assembly Center near Fresno, California. We arrived there in May and stayed for about three months. It was a harsh change from Washington’s cooler climate to the hot Fresno area. The living conditions were tough; we had to fill mattress covers with hay and sleep on steel cots on asphalt floors that sank in the heat. It was a small place compared to Tule Lake, but it was our first experience in the camps.

Pinedale Assembly Center was located near Fresno, California, where we were sent after leaving Washington. Fresno’s climate was very hot compared to what I was used to in Washington, and that was one of the first complaints my parents had. After Pinedale, we also lived near Fresno in Reedley before being sent to Poston. Fresno and its surrounding areas became a significant part of our wartime experience and resettlement.

My father had a farm in Gilroy, California before the war. After we were released from the camps, he had lost the farm—whether it was sold cheap or taken, I’m not sure. We couldn’t return there, so we moved to Reedley where my aunt and uncle lived. The loss of the Gilroy farm was a big blow to my family’s livelihood and future plans. It marked the end of my father’s farming career.

After the camps, we lived in Reedley, California, where my father’s older sister and her Armenian neighbors helped keep the farm going. We stayed with them until my parents found a house there. My father became a custodian at an elementary school and worked part-time at Salwasser manufacturing, while my mother worked as a receptionist and briefly in a hospital. Reedley became our new home after the war, and I attended a country school there without much discrimination.

From Reedley, we were sent straight to the Poston War Relocation Center in Arizona. My mother’s side of the family was also there, so we were together during that time. I was very young, around three to five years old, so I didn’t understand what was happening and thought moving was fun. Poston was one of the main camps where many Japanese Americans were held during the war. It was a difficult period for my family, but I have few memories from that age.

After the war, my father worked part-time at a manufacturing company called Salwasser while mainly working as a custodian at an elementary school. Salwasser was a side job for him to help support the family. This was part of our post-war adjustment as we rebuilt our lives in Reedley. It showed how my father had to adapt to new kinds of work after losing the farm.

My parents were Nisei, second-generation Japanese Americans, while my grandparents were Issei, the first generation. Even though the Nisei generation experienced the camps and discrimination, they rarely talked about it. I think it was cultural or maybe they just wanted to move on. The Nisei generation had a complex identity, caught between loyalty to the U.S. and the hardships they faced during the war.

My grandparents were Issei, the first generation of Japanese immigrants. They were more settled and less willing to move or try new things compared to the Nisei. My father, as an Issei, showed loyalty to America but preferred to stay put rather than move to another camp after Tule Lake. The Issei generation had a different perspective on the war and relocation, often more cautious and reserved.

After Pinedale, we were sent to the Tule Lake War Relocation Center, which was the largest of all the camps with about 10,000 people. I was impressed by how big it was, with many barracks, tall fences, guard towers, and the surrounding landmarks like Castle Rock and Abalone Mountain. Although it was intimidating, I was young and didn’t feel scared, just amazed. Many people from Auburn, including my family and friends, were there in the same ward. Sports like basketball and baseball were our main activities to keep busy.

Castle Rock was one of the landmarks near the Tule Lake War Relocation Center. I remember it clearly because it stood out in the landscape around the camp. It was part of the environment that surrounded us during our time there. Seeing Castle Rock was a reminder of where we were, confined but still connected to the natural world outside.

Abalone Mountain was another landmark near Tule Lake that I remember well. It was part of the scenery around the camp and helped me orient myself in that strange place. The mountain was a constant presence during our stay, along with Castle Rock. These natural features contrasted with the harshness of the camp fences and guard towers.

At Tule Lake, I witnessed conflicts between different groups, including the Kibei, who were Japanese Americans educated in Japan. The loyal Kibei would sometimes go to certain barracks and pull out people they suspected of disloyalty and fight them. These incidents were violent and showed the tensions within the camp community. The Kibei were mostly in their early to mid-twenties and took an active role in enforcing loyalty.

Although I did not go to Topaz War Relocation Center myself, I know it was one of the camps where some people from Tule Lake were sent if they answered 'yes' to the loyalty questionnaire. Some of my family and friends were moved around between camps, but my father chose to stay at Tule Lake rather than move again. Topaz was part of the larger system of camps during the war, representing the difficult choices Japanese Americans faced.
